solutions.solution: The use of 'could' implies a difficulty in obtaining reservation, hence the conclusion is valid. Conclusion II is not certain.
Since all persons are not very well off it must mean that all of them are driven by a desire as mentioned in conclusion II.

Since poor people live in mud houses and mud houses are cooler in summer, it means that poor people would have been more comfortable in summer. But when the people shifted to mud houses and mud houses are cooler in summer, then it means that poor people would have more comfortable in summer. But when the people shifted to air-conditioners, then clearly, the first statement about the statement. Conclusion I is irrelevant to the given statement.
Only one of the two reasons mentioned could stop patients from going to eminent doctors.

The statement says that the user of non-conventional energy sources will replace (eliminate) the energy crisis. It does not say that their replacement is already on. Hence, the first conclusion is invalid. The second is too general to be drawn from such a statement.
The given conclusions are far-fetched and not certain.
Neither of the conclusions is certain. May be only one sprinter made the run.
Although no certain conclusion can be derived because it is not given whether X was hit by a bullet or not, yet one of the two conclusions may be true.
The first does follow because the tone of the statement is that educational value of newspaper is given due importance while its entertainment value is not. Clearly, the second conclusion is invalid.
The first conclusion is irrelevant. The second is the meaning of the given statement. 'Too expensive to be a best friend' means that it can't be bad because it is expensive.
The statement merely says that camels are very useful for deserts. It does not state whether they are cheap or available in large number.
The first conclusion is obviously false. Otherwise there would be too loose and not 'safe', and hence it is not certain.
From the way facts are given, the second conclusion seems valid. But the first conclusion is fallacious as it declares that women are not interested in anything else.
The first conclusion is obviously valid as it is a restatement of the given sentence. The second may or may not be true.
What happens to players who broke records or were not given. Hence, either the player will receive the prize or he will not get it.

Although it is obvious that such players are commonly not given the prize, here we have to concentrate only on the given sentence. I post-conclusions are correct. Killing continues, means the first conclusion is valid. Killing continues while the government says that terrorists are in check, means that the second is valid.

Both conclusions are correct. Shortage means that imports must have been made since aluminium is a metal which can't be done without it as a movement from shortage to self-sufficiency can be achieved and 'this metal is also continued, then even surplus could be reached. Hence, the second conclusion is also valid.
The first conclusion is obviously correct. The second is also correct because the statement talks of human 'weaknesses' and not just a single weakness.
The first conclusion is correct since this is a fact. The second is an irrational inference.
The first is a direct consequence of the given statement. The second is not correct but is a consequence that from an energy crisis.

Both conclusions are valid since Americans have not been able to develop a particular enzyme. It does not mean that they have not developed any enzyme. Similarly, the second conclusion is also invalid. Just because an analogous effect of the enzyme was observed on the two types of proteins, it can't necessarily mean that the proteins have the same composition.

Both conclusions are invalid. Maybe, although the demand is increasing at a faster rate, actual value is less than the production. [For example, India's population is increasing at a faster rate than China's although its population is less than that of China.] In this case, there may be a surplus and hence no need to import. Similarly, instead of reducing demand, there may be a boost in production.
The first conclusion is too severe. The second is directly implied by the statement.

Neither of the conclusions can be said to definitely follow. 'Practically every mineral' does not mean 'every mineral' and hence gold may or may not be there. Similarly, a uranium content of 3 grams per 1000 tonnes of water may or may not harbour radiation hazard.
The author's tone implies that he knows some things but does not know so many more; that he is practically ignorant of everything. This means the conclusion II is valid.
Conclusion I is vague, nothing has been mentioned about 'expressions'. It is obvious.
None of the conclusions is in any way related to the given statement.
The first conclusion is the meaning of the statement. The second is not implied in the statement.
